past_medical_history: Episode of right-sided optic neuritis 8 months ago with painful vision loss lasting 4 weeks, treated with a short course of oral corticosteroids by an ophthalmologist; vision recovered to near-baseline
Iron deficiency anemia diagnosed 3 years ago, resolved with supplementation
Mild intermittent tension-type headaches since college
family_history: Mother with Hashimoto's thyroiditis, diagnosed at age 42
One older sister with Graves' disease
No family history of multiple sclerosis or other neurological diseases

social_history.alcohol: Occasional social drinker, approximately 2-3 drinks per month
social_history.living_situation: Lives alone in a university apartment
social_history.exercise: Regular jogging 3-4 times per week, recreational swimming on weekends

neurological_exam.mental_status: Alert, oriented to person, place, time, and situation. Speech is fluent without dysarthria. Language comprehension and repetition intact. Affect is mildly anxious but cooperative. Mini-Mental State Examination score 30/30.
neurological_exam.cranial_nerves: CN II: Visual acuity 20/25 in the right eye (previously affected), 20/20 in the left eye. No relative afferent pupillary defect at this visit. Subtle red desaturation in the right eye compared to left. Fundoscopy shows mild temporal pallor of the right optic disc. CN III-XII: Leftward gaze-evoked nystagmus present. No facial asymmetry. Tongue midline without fasciculations. Palate elevates symmetrically.
neurological_exam.motor: Upper extremities: 5/5 bilaterally in deltoids, biceps, triceps, wrist extensors, grip, and interossei. Lower extremities: 4+/5 left hip flexion and left knee extension, 5/5 right lower extremity throughout. No pronator drift. No fasciculations or atrophy noted.
neurological_exam.sensory: Decreased vibration sense in both lower extremities to the ankles. Decreased light touch and pinprick over the left lower extremity from the knee distally. Band-like paresthesia reported around the lower chest (approximately T10 dermatomal level) on the left side. Temperature sensation intact. Proprioception mildly reduced in the left great toe.
neurological_exam.reflexes: Biceps 2+ bilaterally. Triceps 2+ bilaterally. Brachioradialis 2+ bilaterally. Patellar 3+ bilaterally (brisk). Achilles 3+ on the left, 2+ on the right. Left plantar response extensor (positive Babinski sign). Right plantar response flexor.
neurological_exam.coordination: Finger-to-nose testing intact bilaterally. Heel-to-shin mildly impaired on the left with slight dysmetria. Rapid alternating movements intact bilaterally.
neurological_exam.gait: Mildly spastic gait with left leg circumduction. Tandem gait unsteady with lateral sway. Romberg sign positive. Able to walk on heels and toes but heel-walking slightly impaired on the left.
neurological_exam.additional: Lhermitte sign positive (electric shock sensation radiating down the spine and into the legs with neck flexion). Uhthoff phenomenon reported by patient: symptoms worsen after hot showers. EDSS estimated at 3.0.

patient.chief_complaint: Progressive left leg weakness and numbness over 10 days with unsteady gait
patient.history_present_illness: Ms. Wei Chen is a 27-year-old right-handed East Asian woman who presents with a 10-day history of progressive weakness and numbness in her left lower extremity accompanied by gait unsteadiness. She first noticed a tingling sensation in her left foot approximately two weeks ago while studying, which she initially attributed to prolonged sitting. Over the following several days, the numbness ascended to involve her entire left lower leg up to the knee, and she began to notice a tight, band-like sensation around her lower chest on the left side. Concurrently, she developed progressive weakness in her left leg, manifesting as difficulty climbing stairs, a tendency for her left foot to catch on uneven surfaces, and a sense that her leg was stiff and heavy. By the time of presentation she was having difficulty walking more than a block without stumbling and felt unsafe on stairs. She also reports that for the past several days she has noticed worsening of her symptoms after taking hot showers, with increased leg heaviness and blurred vision in the right eye that resolves after cooling down. On further questioning, she recalls an episode approximately eight months ago when she experienced painful vision loss in her right eye over several days, which was diagnosed as optic neuritis by her ophthalmologist and treated with a short course of oral prednisone. Her vision recovered over about four weeks, though she notes that colors still appear slightly washed out in the right eye compared to the left. She additionally reports persistent fatigue over the past several months that is disproportionate to her activity level and has affected her academic performance. She has noticed occasional urinary urgency, needing to rush to the bathroom more frequently than before, but denies frank incontinence. She denies fevers, weight loss, rash, joint pain, oral ulcers, dysphagia, or recent infections. She has no history of travel outside of her home city in the past year and denies any tick bites or exposure to animals.

findings.location: Periventricular white matter, bilateral, oriented perpendicular to the lateral ventricles
findings.size: Multiple lesions ranging from 4 mm to 15 mm

signal_characteristics.T1: Several lesions hypointense (T1 black holes indicating chronic axonal damage)
signal_characteristics.FLAIR: Hyperintense, ovoid morphology perpendicular to ventricles consistent with Dawson fingers

findings.location: Juxtacortical white matter, left frontal and right parietal lobes
Right middle cerebellar peduncle and dorsal pons (infratentorial)
Thoracic spinal cord at T9-T10, predominantly dorsal columns, left-sided

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

findings.location: Left periventricular white matter, adjacent to the body of the left lateral ventricle

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Open ring enhancement pattern, ring open toward cortex

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C2-C3, posterior columns
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (all spinal cord lesions span one vertebral segment or less)
mri.impression: Multiple demyelinating lesions involving periventricular white matter (Dawson fingers morphology), juxtacortical white matter, infratentorial structures (right cerebellar peduncle and dorsal pons), and spinal cord (cervical C2-C3 and thoracic T9-T10). Two lesions demonstrate gadolinium enhancement (left periventricular and thoracic cord), indicating active inflammation. Findings satisfy the 2017 McDonald criteria for dissemination in space (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, and spinal cord involvement) and dissemination in time (simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions). Clinical correlation recommended.

special_tests.Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 5 bands unique to CSF, not present in matched serum sample

csf.interpretation: Opening pressure: 14 cmH2O. WBC: 12 cells/microL (reference: 0-5) (N/A PMN/N/A lymph). Protein: 44.2 mg/dL (reference: 15-45 mg/dL). Glucose: 58.3 mg/dL (reference: 40-70 mg/dL) (ratio 0.64 (serum glucose 91 mg/dL)). Oligoclonal bands: Positive — 5 bands unique to CSF, not present in matched serum sample. IgG index: 0.78 (elevated; reference: <0.70). IgG synthesis rate: 10.8 mg/day (elevated; reference: <8.0 mg/day). Myelin basic protein: 5.2 ng/mL (elevated; reference: <4.0 ng/mL). Cytology: No malignant cells identified.

output.impression: Abnormal values: Right eye P100 latency 118 ms ms (H), Right eye P100 amplitude 4.6 microV microV (H), Interocular P100 latency difference 20 ms ms (H).

findings.location: Cervical spinal cord at C2-C3, posterior columns
findings.size: Less than one vertebral segment in craniocaudal extent

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord

findings.location: Thoracic spinal cord at T9-T10, dorsal columns, predominantly left-sided

signal_characteristics.T2: Hyperintense, involving less than half the cross-sectional area of the cord
signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: Nodular enhancement within the lesion, indicating active inflammation

findings.mass_effect: Mild focal cord swelling at T9-T10

additional_observations: No longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (both lesions span one vertebral segment or less)
No cord atrophy at this time
No compressive lesions or disc herniation
Normal conus medullaris and cauda equina
output.impression: Lesion characteristics are typical of demyelinating disease with MS pattern (short segment, peripheral location, less than half cross-sectional area). Clinical correlation recommended.

Abnormal values: Peripapillary RNFL thickness (average) 70 micrometers micrometers (H), RNFL temporal quadrant 45 micrometers micrometers (H), Ganglion cell-inner plexiform layer (GCIPL) 65 micrometers micrometers (H).

findings.size: Multiple lesions, unchanged compared to prior study

signal_characteristics.T1_post_contrast: No enhancement (previously enhancing left periventricular lesion no longer enhances)

findings.location: Left juxtacortical white matter, temporal lobe

volumetrics.new_lesions: 1 new enhancing lesion in left temporal juxtacortical white matter
volumetrics.resolving_lesions: 2 previously enhancing lesions no longer enhance

additional_observations: Previously noted spinal cord and brain lesions are stable
No evidence of progressive brain atrophy
output.impression: Follow-up MRI at 3 months shows one new small enhancing lesion in the left temporal juxtacortical white matter, indicating ongoing subclinical disease activity. Previously enhancing lesions have resolved. Clinical correlation recommended.

output.proposed: Ocrelizumab 300 mg IV infusion every 6 months
contraindications: Active hepatitis B infection (screen before initiation)
Known hypersensitivity to ocrelizumab or murine proteins
warnings: Infusion-related reactions common with first dose — premedicate with methylprednisolone 100 mg IV, diphenhydramine, and acetaminophen
Increased risk of infections due to B-cell depletion — screen for hepatitis B and tuberculosis before starting
Live or live-attenuated vaccines should be administered at least 6 weeks before initiation
Monitor immunoglobulin levels periodically during treatment
Discuss contraception — limited pregnancy data, recommend effective contraception during treatment and for 6 months after last dose
output.formulary_status: Formulary, prior authorization required, specialty pharmacy distribution
alternatives: Natalizumab (high efficacy, requires JCV antibody monitoring, risk of PML)
Dimethyl fumarate (moderate efficacy, oral, monitor lymphocytes)
Fingolimod (moderate-high efficacy, oral, first-dose cardiac monitoring required)
Glatiramer acetate (lower efficacy, injection, favorable safety profile)

results.title: Diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: 2017 revisions of the McDonald criteria
results.authors: Thompson AJ, Banwell BL, Barkhof F, et al.
results.key_findings: Revised criteria allow CSF-specific oligoclonal bands to substitute for dissemination in time. Both symptomatic and asymptomatic MRI lesions can be used for DIS and DIT. Cortical lesions can substitute for juxtacortical lesions for DIS.

results.title: MRI criteria for the diagnosis of multiple sclerosis: MAGNIMS consensus guidelines
results.authors: Filippi M, Rocca MA, Ciccarelli O, et al.
results.key_findings: Dissemination in space requires at least 1 T2 lesion in at least 2 of 4 CNS areas: periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord.

output.summary: The 2017 McDonald criteria for MS diagnosis require demonstration of dissemination in space (DIS) and dissemination in time (DIT). DIS requires one or more T2 lesions in at least 2 of 4 characteristic CNS areas. DIT can be demonstrated by simultaneous presence of enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single MRI, new T2/enhancing lesions on follow-up MRI, or CSF-specific oligoclonal bands. In this patient, DIS is met with lesions in all 4 characteristic locations (periventricular, juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord), and DIT is satisfied by simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on MRI, as well as by the presence of CSF oligoclonal bands.

output.query: NMOSD versus multiple sclerosis differentiation diagnostic criteria

results.title: International consensus diagnostic criteria for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders
results.authors: Wingerchuk DM, Banwell B, Bennett JL, et al.
results.key_findings: NMOSD diagnosis requires at least 1 core clinical characteristic (optic neuritis, acute myelitis, area postrema syndrome, brainstem syndrome, diencephalic syndrome, cerebral syndrome) plus AQP4-IgG seropositivity. AQP4-IgG seronegative NMOSD requires additional clinical and MRI criteria.

results.title: Differentiating multiple sclerosis from AQP4-neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder and MOG-antibody disease
results.key_findings: Key distinguishing features of NMOSD include longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>3 vertebral segments), area postrema syndrome, optic neuritis with prominent disc edema, and absence of periventricular Dawson fingers. MS typically shows short-segment cord lesions, periventricular ovoid lesions, and CSF oligoclonal bands.

output.summary: Differentiating MS from NMOSD is critical because treatment approaches differ significantly. Key MS features include periventricular Dawson fingers, short-segment spinal cord lesions (<2 vertebral segments), CSF oligoclonal bands, and negative AQP4-IgG. Key NMOSD features include longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (>3 vertebral segments), area postrema syndrome with intractable nausea/vomiting/hiccups, AQP4-IgG seropositivity, and absence of typical MS-pattern brain lesions. In this patient, the periventricular Dawson fingers pattern, short-segment cord lesions, positive oligoclonal bands, and negative AQP4-IgG strongly favor MS over NMOSD.

differential.key_features: AQP4 antibodies negative, short-segment spinal cord lesions rather than longitudinally extensive, periventricular Dawson fingers morphology typical of MS not NMOSD, no area postrema syndrome
MOG antibodies negative, optic disc pallor rather than prominent edema, CSF oligoclonal bands (less common in MOGAD), periventricular Dawson fingers pattern typical of MS
Normal ESR/CRP, negative ANA, Dawson fingers morphology inconsistent with vasculitis, age and sex demographic more typical of MS
No leptomeningeal enhancement, no systemic findings (pulmonary, skin, lymphadenopathy), lesion distribution classic for MS not sarcoidosis

differential.diagnosis: Systemic lupus erythematosus with CNS involvement
differential.key_features: Negative ANA, no systemic features (rash, arthralgia, serositis), normal ESR and CRP, MRI pattern typical of MS

optimal_actions.action: Obtain detailed history including prior neurological symptoms suggesting prior demyelinating events (dissemination in time)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: History of right optic neuritis 8 months ago provides clinical evidence of a prior demyelinating event

optimal_actions.action: Perform comprehensive neurological examination with attention to long tract signs, sensory levels, and residual optic nerve dysfunction
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Left lower extremity weakness, sensory level at T10, left Babinski sign, Lhermitte sign, residual right optic disc pallor and red desaturation, gaze-evoked nystagmus

optimal_actions.action: Order MRI brain and spinal cord with gadolinium contrast
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Multiple demyelinating lesions meeting McDonald 2017 DIS criteria across all 4 characteristic locations, with enhancing and non-enhancing lesions meeting DIT criteria

optimal_actions.action: Order basic labs including CBC, BMP, ESR, CRP, ANA, vitamin B12, NMO-IgG, and MOG antibodies to exclude mimics
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Normal labs except low vitamin D; negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies excluding NMOSD and MOGAD

optimal_actions.action: Perform lumbar puncture with CSF analysis including oligoclonal bands and IgG index
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Oligoclonal bands present, elevated IgG index and synthesis rate, mild lymphocytic pleocytosis, elevated myelin basic protein

optimal_actions.action: Initiate IV methylprednisolone 1g daily for 3-5 days for acute myelitis relapse
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Expected acceleration of recovery from acute spinal cord relapse

optimal_actions.action: Discuss diagnosis of relapsing-remitting MS with patient and counsel on disease-modifying therapy options
Initiate disease-modifying therapy (consider high-efficacy agent such as ocrelizumab given active disease with multiple relapses)
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Selected DMT appropriate for patient profile, disease activity, and risk tolerance

optimal_actions.action: Order visual evoked potentials to document residual optic nerve demyelination from prior episode
optimal_actions.expected_finding: Prolonged P100 latency in right eye confirming prior demyelinating optic neuropathy

optimal_actions.action: Order optical coherence tomography to quantify retinal nerve fiber layer thinning
optimal_actions.expected_finding: RNFL thinning in the right eye consistent with prior optic neuritis

optimal_actions.action: Supplement vitamin D (2000-5000 IU daily) given low levels
Schedule follow-up MRI in 3 months to establish new baseline on disease-modifying therapy

critical_actions: Obtain brain and spinal cord MRI with gadolinium to evaluate for demyelinating lesions and confirm dissemination in space and time
Perform lumbar puncture to assess for oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index
Check NMO-IgG (aquaporin-4 antibodies) and MOG antibodies to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD
Treat acute myelitis relapse with IV methylprednisolone
Initiate disease-modifying therapy after confirming MS diagnosis
contraindicated_actions: Do not start chronic oral corticosteroids as maintenance therapy for MS (not effective for relapse prevention and harmful long-term)
Do not perform brain biopsy — diagnosis is achievable with non-invasive McDonald criteria
Do not delay disease-modifying therapy while waiting for spontaneous remission of the current relapse
Do not initiate natalizumab without first checking JC virus antibody status
Do not administer live vaccines while planning B-cell-depleting therapy (e.g., ocrelizumab)
key_reasoning_points: The combination of a current acute myelitis episode with a prior episode of optic neuritis 8 months ago establishes clinical dissemination in time across two distinct CNS sites
MRI demonstrates dissemination in space across all 4 characteristic MS locations: periventricular (Dawson fingers), juxtacortical, infratentorial (cerebellar peduncle), and spinal cord
Simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on MRI provide radiographic dissemination in time, satisfying 2017 McDonald criteria independent of clinical history
CSF oligoclonal bands and elevated IgG index provide additional evidence of intrathecal immune activation and can substitute for DIT under 2017 McDonald criteria
Negative AQP4 and MOG antibodies are essential to exclude NMOSD and MOGAD, which have different treatment approaches and prognoses

The source case presented with area postrema syndrome (nausea, vomiting, hiccups) and AQP4-IgG positivity consistent with NMOSD, but this patient's periventricular Dawson fingers, short-segment cord lesions, and negative AQP4 antibodies clearly distinguish MS from NMOSD
Lhermitte sign (electric sensation with neck flexion) and Uhthoff phenomenon (symptom worsening with heat) are classic clinical features of MS-related spinal cord demyelination
Early initiation of high-efficacy DMT is recommended given two clinical relapses within 8 months and MRI evidence of active and subclinical disease

metadata.difficulty_description: Straightforward case with classic MS presentation: prior optic neuritis followed by acute myelitis, classic MRI findings (Dawson fingers, short-segment cord lesions, enhancing and non-enhancing lesions), positive CSF oligoclonal bands, and negative NMOSD/MOGAD antibodies. Minimal confounders.
